title: Explicit argument indexing in std::format should be preferred only for non-trivial ordering
description: Explicit argument indexing in "std::format" should be used only for non-trivial orderingAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode Smellclumsysince-c++20Why is this an issue?Calls tostd::formatandstd::vformatcan receive either format strings whose replacement fields are fully indexed or fully non-indexed.Explicitly indexing replacement fields is useful when arguments are not used in order or are used multiple times. Implicit indexing has the advantage of being terse and simple.Using explicit indexing in a context where implicit indexing would have the same behavior is more verbose and error-prone. It might confuse the reader, who might expect the arguments not to be used in order.This rule raises an issue on explicit indexing that should be replaced by implicit indexing.Nonco
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: std::string message = std::format("{0} {1} {2}", name, age, city); // Noncompliant: sequential indexing
std::string info = std::format("Hello {0}!", username); // Noncompliant: single argument with index
std::string result = std::format("{0}: {1}, {2}: {3}", "Name", name, "Age", age); // Noncompliant: sequential
compliant: std::string message = std::format("{} {} {}", name, age, city); // Compliant: implicit indexing
std::string info = std::format("Hello {}!", username); // Compliant: implicit for single arg
std::string repeated = std::format("{0} and {0} again", value); // Compliant: argument used multiple times
std::string reordered = std::format("{1} comes before {0}", first, second); // Compliant: non-sequential order
std::string mixed = std::format("{}: {}, {}: {}", "Name", name, "Age", age); // Compliant: implicit sequential

checks.message: Explicit argument indexing in std::format should be preferred only for non-trivial ordering.
